query: Strait of Hormuz shipping transit June 2026 current status reopening negotiations
answer:
This is an extraordinarily detailed finding. Let me compile the final research report.

---

# Strait of Hormuz Shipping Transit — Status as of June 19, 2026

## Executive Summary

The Strait of Hormuz has been **effectively closed to all commercial shipping since February 28, 2026** — approximately 111 days. S&P Global has called it **"the biggest disruption in the history of modern energy."** A diplomatic breakthrough — the **Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding** — was digitally signed on June 14, 2026, with a **formal signing ceremony scheduled for today, June 19, at the Buergenstock Resort in Switzerland**. However, actual commercial transit remains at effectively zero due to IRGC non-endorsement, naval mines, ongoing nightly drone attacks, and frozen insurance markets.

---

## 1. Why the Strait Closed

- **February 28, 2026**: The US and Israel launched **"Operation Midnight Hammer"** — coordinated strikes killing Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, destroying nuclear facilities at Fordow, Natanz, and Isfahan, hitting over 11,000 targets, and killing 3,468+ Iranians.
- Iran retaliated by **closing the Strait of Hormuz** to all commercial shipping.
- **Mojtaba Khamenei** (son of the deceased leader) was named new Supreme Leader on March 8–9. As of June 16, he had not made a verified public appearance in 99+ days; US intelligence reports severe injuries.

## 2. Current Closure Status

| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Commercial vessel traffic | **Effectively zero** | S&P Global |
| Days of closure | **~111 days** (as of Jun 19) | Reuters |
| Ships stranded | **~2,000** | Reuters, Wikipedia |
| Mariners stranded | **~20,000 from 87 countries** | Al Jazeera, Wikipedia |
| Pre-war daily throughput | **~20.5 million bbl/day** | EIA |
| Share of world seaborne oil | **~25%** | EIA |
| Share of global LNG | **~20%** | EIA |

- On **June 10–11**, the IRGC formally declared **"complete closure"** and published expanded boundary maps extending into UAE territorial waters.
- As of **June 17**, Iran was **firing multiple drones at ships in the Strait every night**; the US Navy has been intercepting them.

## 3. The Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding (MOU)

### Negotiation Timeline
- **Feb 6–26**: Three rounds of pre-war talks (Muscat, Geneva)
- **Feb 28**: War begins — talks collapse
- **Apr 7–8**: Two-week ceasefire brokered by Pakistan
- **Apr 11–12**: First direct US-Iran talks since 1979 in Islamabad (21 hours; collapsed)
- **Apr 13**: US imposes naval blockade of Iran
- **May 22**: Qatar joins Pakistan as co-mediator
- **Jun 14**: **MOU digitally signed by both sides**
- **Jun 19 (today)**: **Formal signing ceremony — Buergenstock Resort, Switzerland**

### Key MOU Terms (as reported)
1. End to military strikes between US and Iran
2. **Reopening of the Strait of Hormuz — immediately, without tolls**
3. Full restoration of pre-war shipping within ~30 days
4. End to the US naval blockade of Iranian ports
5. 60-day ceasefire extension for further negotiations
6. $300 billion investment fund for Iran (disputed — Trump called it "Fake News")
7. Release of frozen Iranian assets (Iran claims $24B; US denies)
8. Iran agrees not to produce/acquire nuclear weapons and to dilute HEU stockpile

### Competing Interpretations
- Trump claims the Strait will be "permanently toll-free"; Iran says it will charge "fees"
- Iran claims immediate sanctions relief; the US denies this
- VP Vance described the MOU as **"a very general document" roughly "a page and a half" long**
- Full text has not been publicly released

## 4. Obstacles to Physical Reopening

1. **IRGC non-endorsement**: The IRGC operationally controls the closure and has NOT publicly endorsed the deal — they declared "complete closure" just 3–4 days before it was signed.
2. **Nightly drone attacks**: Iran firing multiple drones at Hormuz shipping every night as of June 17.
3. **Naval mines**: Mine-clearing operations required; France, Britain, and others announced minesweeper deployments (NYT, June 16).
4. **Insurance markets frozen**: No commercial insurance coverage available for Hormuz transit.
5. **Israeli opposition**: Netanyahu struck Beirut on June 14 (same day as deal); Israeli Security Cabinet may formally oppose.
6. **Congressional opposition**: 52 US senators and 177 representatives signed a letter opposing any deal allowing continued enrichment.
7. **Fitch assessment**: Broad Hormuz reopening unlikely before **July 2026** at the earliest.

## 5. Oil Market & Economic Impact

| Metric | Value | Date |
|--------|-------|------|
| Brent crude (pre-war) | ~$72.50/bbl | Feb 27 |
| Brent crude (peak) | **$126.10/bbl** (+74%) | Late April |
| Brent crude (post-deal) | $83.63/bbl | Jun 15 |
| US gasoline (pre-war) | $2.98/gal | Feb 26 |
| US gasoline (peak) | **$4.45/gal** (+49%) | May 3 |
| Global oil supply loss | ~2 million bpd | IEA est. |
| Iran daily blockade loss | ~$435 million/day | Apr–Jun |
| US war cost | ~$27 billion | Through May |
| US SPR release | 17.5 million barrels | Mar–Apr |
| Q1 US PCE inflation | 4.5% annualized | Q1 2026 |
| Euro area energy inflation | 10.9% | April 2026 |
| Goldman Sachs recession prob. | 30% (12-month) | Jun 2026 |

## 6. Prediction Markets (as of June 15)

| Market | Question | Probability |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| Metaculus #44056 | US & Iran formally sign before June 26? | **75%** |
| Metaculus #44050 | Iran reopen Strait before June 27? | **40%** |
| Polymarket | US-Iran sign agreement by July 31 | **~81.5%** |

The **35-point gap** between signing probability (75%) and reopening probability (40%) reflects deep market skepticism that a signed deal will translate into actual physical reopening.

## 7. Military Situation Summary

- **US forces deployed**: 3 Carrier Strike Groups, 2 Marine Expeditionary Units ("Project Freedom")
- **US naval blockade of Iran**: Imposed April 13, 2026
- **US casualties**: 15 killed, 543 wounded
- **Iranian casualties**: 3,468+ killed
- **Key June incidents**: Iran shot down US Apache helicopter (Jun 9); US struck Bandar Abbas/Qeshm Island (Jun 10–11); 60-day ceasefire extension announced Jun 11

## Bottom Line

The Strait of Hormuz remains **physically closed** despite a diplomatic framework being signed. The formal ceremony is today (June 19) but three critical barriers — IRGC non-compliance with nightly drone attacks, uncleared naval mines, and frozen insurance markets — mean **meaningful commercial traffic restoration likely extends into July 2026 at the earliest**. The deal itself is described as "very general" with the US and Iran publicly contradicting each other on key terms including tolls, sanctions relief, and asset releases.

key_facts: The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed to all commercial shipping since February 28, 2026 (~111 days as of June 19), following US-Israeli 'Operation Midnight Hammer' strikes on Iran
S&P Global called this 'the biggest disruption in the history of modern energy' — the Strait normally handles ~20.5 million bbl/day (~25% of world seaborne oil, ~20% of global LNG)
Approximately 2,000 ships and 20,000 mariners from 87 countries remain stranded
The Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding was digitally signed June 14, 2026 by both the US and Iran, with a formal signing ceremony scheduled for June 19 at the Buergenstock Resort, Switzerland

Key MOU terms include: immediate Strait reopening without tolls, full shipping restoration within ~30 days, 60-day ceasefire extension, end to US naval blockade, and Iran agreeing not to produce nuclear weapons
VP Vance described the MOU as 'a very general document' roughly 'a page and a half' long; full text has not been publicly released
The US and Iran publicly contradict each other on key terms: Trump says 'permanently toll-free' vs Iran says it will charge 'fees'; Iran claims $24B asset release vs US denies
The IRGC has NOT endorsed the deal and declared 'complete closure' on June 10-11, just days before the MOU was signed
As of June 17, Iran was firing multiple drones at ships in the Strait every night; the US Navy is intercepting them
Naval mines must be cleared before commercial transit can resume; France, Britain, and others announced minesweeper deployments
Commercial insurance coverage for Hormuz transit remains unavailable (markets frozen)
Fitch assesses broad Hormuz reopening is unlikely before July 2026
Brent crude peaked at $126.10/bbl (+74% from pre-war $72.50) in late April, falling to $83.63 post-deal on June 15
US gasoline peaked at $4.45/gal (+49% from $2.98 pre-war) on May 3
Prediction markets show a 35-point gap: 75% probability of formal signing vs only 40% probability of actual Strait reopening before June 27
Key mediators: Pakistan (primary, PM Sharif), Qatar (co-mediator from May 22), with Saudi Arabia and Turkey also involved
52 US senators and 177 representatives signed a letter opposing any deal allowing continued enrichment
US military presence: 3 Carrier Strike Groups, 2 Marine Expeditionary Units; US casualties: 15 killed, 543 wounded; US war cost ~$27 billion through May
